problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem A (continuous): build a realistic, sensible model of the spread of the West African Ebola outbreak that captures both the disease dynamics AND the eradication problem — specifically the optimal delivery of a newly announced medicine, the quantity needed, and the speed of manufacture, with the deliverable framed as advice to the World Medical Association (WMA). This O-award paper (#40764) reframes the open prompt into a compartmental epidemic model with three population states (Infected I, vulnerable-healthy P, immune-healthy H) plus birth/death, parameterizes it from real WHO/CountryMeters data for Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone and the region as a whole, validates against 85 days of WHO data, then runs sensitivity analysis on three concrete interventions (medical services, medicine, vaccine) over short/medium/long horizons to produce country-targeted policy recommendations.
models_used: Custom SIR-style compartmental epidemic model with 3 states (Infected I, vulnerable-healthy P, immune-healthy H) — an open system with births/deaths, parameterized by 6 transition rates (alpha infection, beta recovery, gamma infected-death, nu vaccination, phi healthy-death, epsilon birth)

Stochastic (continuous-time Markov / Gillespie-style) simulation: per-timestep transition probabilities p_i = rate*population*dt with a no-event residual probability 1-sum(p_i), event chosen by a uniform random draw r in [0,1] over the cumulative-probability ranges; timestep dt chosen so sum(p_i)<=1 (at most one event per step)
Deterministic discrete-time dynamical system: coupled nonlinear difference equations f,g,h for P_{n+1}, I_{n+1}, H_{n+1} using the same rate parameters, iterated daily

Fixed-point / equilibrium analysis: solved the difference system for all 8 steady states (extinction, all-immune, all-infected, all-vulnerable, disease-free with immunity, endemic, etc.) tabulated with parameter conditions

Linear stability analysis via the 3x3 Jacobian and the Jury stability conditions |tr(J)| < 1+det(J) < 2 for discrete maps, applied to the disease-free 'Case 6' fixed point to derive the eradication condition nu > 1 - phi

Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis: averaging 10 stochastic runs per scenario, reporting standardized errors |(model-WHO)/sigma| and per-intervention net change in cases/deaths at 6 months / 1 year / 2 years
key_techniques: Mechanistic parameter derivation from real data rather than free fitting: epsilon=BirthRate/Pop, phi=DeathRate/Pop, mortality=TotalDeaths/TotalCases, then gamma=mortality/T and beta=(1-mortality)/T using a 'disease residence time' T, and alpha=K/(T*P0) using K = avg secondary infections per case

Reducing the hard-to-measure transmission rate alpha to two physically interpretable surrogate parameters T (residence time) and K (secondary infections), then calibrating T,K to fit WHO data while cross-checking K against an external CDC/Meltzer study (K=0.12 hospitalized, 0.18 isolated-home, 1.8 unisolated) to prove the fitted values are physically plausible

Dual-model strategy that exploits complementary strengths: stochastic model for integer populations, randomness, near-continuous time and short/medium-term sensitivity; deterministic model for cheap iteration and closed-form steady-state/stability analysis — and validating the deterministic model indirectly by matching it to the already-validated stochastic one

Mapping each policy lever onto a specific parameter: improve medical services -> decrease alpha (toward K_min=0.54 from a 75/25 incubation argument), improve medicine -> decrease gamma & increase beta (lower mortality to 30%/15%), vaccine -> increase nu (with nu=V/730 for fraction V vaccinated over 2 years)
Quantitative validation with a statistical yardstick: standardized residual |(C_M-C_W)/sigma_C| across 8 statistics, noting 5 of 8 within 0.5 sigma and none beyond 1.1 sigma

Efficiency framing: plotting net death reduction vs percentage increase in treatment, using a trendline so interventions 'above the line' are deemed cost-efficient (an explicit proxy in lieu of real cost data)

Analytic eradication result: collapsing the stability inequality to nu > 1 - phi using the limit P_n -> infinity (so 1/M -> 0), proving vaccination is the ONLY lever that guarantees long-term eradication
why_it_won: "It turns a vague, open-ended prompt into a tight, defensible quantitative argument with a single clear punchline backed three different ways. (1) Real-data discipline: nearly every parameter is grounded in WHO/CountryMeters numbers and cross-validated against external literature (Meltzer/CDC for K), so judges trust the model rather than suspecting curve-fitting. (2) Honest, rigorous validation: it compares to 85 days of real data across four regions and reports standardized errors, not just eyeballed curves. (3) Analytical depth that most teams skip — full fixed-point enumeration plus Jury-condition stability analysis yields a crisp, citable theorem-like result (nu > 1 - phi => 'a vaccine is the only guarantee of eradication'), which is exactly the kind of non-obvious, decision-relevant insight judges reward. (4) Decision-usefulness: results are disaggregated by country and time horizon and converted into concrete, prioritized recommendations, then delivered in the required WMA letter. (5) Intellectual honesty: it explicitly flags that it ignored cost and that residence time T may be an 'artefact', which builds credibility instead of overclaiming."
section_highlights: "Summary: leads with the modeling approach AND the five qualitative findings as bullets, so a judge gets the whole story in one page. Assumptions (Sec 1.1.1): ~13 explicitly stated and individually justified assumptions (binary instantaneous states, homogeneous population, no mutation, complete immunity, constant treatment practices) — each tied to why it is reasonable. Model build (Sec 2): the stochastic transition law is given as a single clean piecewise system (Eq 1) listing all 8 events with probabilities and outcomes; a Table 3 explicitly contrasts stochastic vs deterministic strengths to justify using both. Parameterization (Sec 3.1): a transparent, reproducible chain from raw WHO tables to every parameter, with the external K cross-check. Validation (Sec 3.2): standardized-error table plus per-country day-by-day overlay plots, and a clever indirect validation of the deterministic model against the stochastic one. Sensitivity (Sec 4): a clean three-intervention x three-horizon x three-country grid of net-change tables, then efficiency scatter plots with a trendline. Theory (Sec 4.2.2): full Jacobian, trace, determinant, Jury condition, and the derivation collapsing to nu>1-phi. Self-critique (Sec 5.1): explicit Strengths/Weaknesses bullet lists and concrete Future Work (time-varying parameters, cost function, more compartments, with the note that each added state doubles the fixed points). Closes with the required WMA letter restating recommendations in plain language."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The transmission rate alpha is not measured — it is fitted via T and K to match WHO data, and the paper itself admits T 'comes as an artefact of other aspects of the model' (Liberia's T=155 days is physically implausible), so calibration is partly cosmetic

Heavy reliance on a single anecdote (one survivor's timeline) to justify residence time T, and on WHO data the paper concedes is underestimated — validation window (85 days) is short relative to the full outbreak
Initial conditions are crudely set: H0=0 (no recoveries counted as immune) and I0 = TotalCases - TotalDeaths assumes every non-fatal case is still actively infectious, which overestimates I0

Constant-rate / homogeneous-mixing assumptions ignore spatial structure, incubation latency, super-spreading, behavior change and time-varying treatment — the model trends are 'more linear' than reality by construction

The deterministic long-term forecast diverges to infinity (infected and immune both -> infinity, Fig 6) because births are unbounded and there's no saturating/logistic term — physically the open-system formulation is unrealistic over long horizons

The headline eradication condition nu > 1 - phi is derived only by taking P_n -> infinity (so 1/M->0); with phi tiny, this implies nu near 1 (vaccinate ~everyone per day), so the 'only a vaccine works' result is more a structural artifact of the model than an actionable threshold

No cost model at all — efficiency is proxied by 'deaths reduced per % change in treatment' with an arbitrary trendline; the paper admits real cost-benefit conclusions 'are likely to be quite different'
Sensitivity analysis is coarse (mostly 10% changes, only 10 Monte Carlo runs per scenario, occasional positive case-changes attributed to 'randomness') — under-powered for firm policy ranking
Did not directly model the problem's specific asks about manufacturing speed / quantity of the medicine in physical units; medicine is abstracted purely as a mortality-rate reduction
reusable_patterns: Three-state compartmental skeleton (Infected / Susceptible / Immune) as an open system with birth-death, parameterized by named transition rates — a template for any spread/diffusion/epidemic-flavored continuous problem

Pair a stochastic Gillespie-style simulator with a deterministic ODE/difference-equation version of the SAME rate parameters: use the stochastic one for realism + sensitivity and the deterministic one for cheap iteration and analytic steady-state/stability — then validate the deterministic model against the stochastic one

Calibrate hard-to-measure rates through interpretable physical surrogates (residence time, secondary-infection number) and always cross-check fitted values against an external published study to defend plausibility
Map every policy lever explicitly onto one model parameter (intervention -> parameter change), making sensitivity analysis a clean one-knob-at-a-time experiment
Report validation with standardized residuals |(model-data)/sigma| rather than vague 'good agreement', and overlay model vs data per region

Do the full discrete-time stability toolkit: enumerate all fixed points in a table with parameter conditions, build the Jacobian, and apply the Jury conditions to extract a single closed-form control/eradication threshold — this yields the kind of crisp insight judges love

Structure the sensitivity results as an intervention x time-horizon x region grid of net-change tables, then distill into an efficiency scatter (benefit vs % effort) with a trendline to rank options visually
Always include an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses list and concrete Future Work, and acknowledge omitted factors (here: cost) up front to build credibility
Deliver the required real-world artifact (the WMA letter) in plain decision-maker language, restating the technical findings as prioritized, actionable recommendations
